w:r.w:t: shaped association between daily temperature and mortality; deaths rise at both hot and cold extremes relative to a minimum mortality temperature; heatwaves greatly amplify risk.pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih+2
Strong – Azhar et al., 2014, Environmental Research (Ahmedabad DLNM heat–mortality); Gasparrini et al., 2015, Lancet (global non
linear DLNM ER curves and heatwave RRs for multiple Indian cities. pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih+2
, vasodilation and hypotension, triggering ischemia, arrhythmia and stroke; cold increases blood pressure and thrombosis; extremes trigger CVD deaths. pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih+1
et al., 2017, Environment International (systematic review of heatwaves and cardiovascular mortality).
Yes – pooled RRs by temperature/heatwave; city
level ER functions can be derived/approximated. pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih+1

Heatwaves and cold spells exacerbate COPD and pneumonia via airway irritation, infections and pollutant interactions; vulnerable groups include elderly and chronic lung disease patients. pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih+1
analytic RRs for respiratory mortality during heatwaves/extremes.
Higher ambient temperature increases microbial growth and reduces water quality; with heavy rainfall/flooding this causes contamination and increased diarrhoea, especially in
Moderate–strong – Bhavnani et al., 2014, Global Environmental Change (“Climate change and waterborne diarrhoea in northern India”).
based ER: % change in diarrhoea prevalence per increase in weekly average temperature (15–30 °C).

Yes – climate–transmission models provide RRs for dengue incidence vs. temperature and warming scenarios.

cycle and extends transmission season/areas, up to species
Strong – Dhiman et al., 2010/2011, Indian Journal of Medical Research (sections on malaria and climate change in India).
dependent transmission models and projections for India. pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih+1
Repeated occupational exposure to extreme heat and dehydration in agricultural communities causes recurrent subclinical acute kidney injury → chronic tubulo
Emerging–moderate – Wesseling et al., 2017, Kidney International Reports (“
in agricultural communities: A case against heat stress nephropathy?”).
°C RR yet; mostly ecological/occupational data – suitable for exploratory or scenario EBOD only.
Direct thermal load overwhelms thermoregulation; hyperthermia, multiorgan failure, CNS dysfunction, especially during heatwaves in outdoor workers and elderly.who+1
Strong – Kumar et al., 2024, Annals of National Academy of Medical Sciences (heatstroke series, North India).
term spikes in temperature above city
specific thresholds increase deaths via heat stroke and decompensation of chronic disease; DLNM/GAM show U/J
Strong – Azhar et al., 2014, Environmental Research (Ahmedabad DLNM heat–mortality).
Mortality increases at both hot and cold extremes relative to minimum mortality temperature; non
Strong – Gasparrini et al., 2015, Lancet (global non

Strong – Ghosh et al., 2024, Sci Total Environ or similar multi
, tachycardia, hypotension; cold → higher BP, thrombosis; both extremes trigger ischemia, arrhythmia, stroke, especially in elderly/comorbid.pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih+1

term temperature variability and extremes increase hospital admissions for IHD, heart failure and
Moderate – Li et al., 2019, PLOS Medicine (multi
country study on temperature variability and hospital admissions).
Temperature controls Aedes survival, biting rate and viral extrinsic incubation; warming (to an optimum) boosts transmission intensity and outbreaks.pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih+1
Strong – Dhiman et al., 2010, Indian Journal of Medical Research (“Climate change and threats of vector
Temperature shortens parasite development time and modifies Anopheles survival and biting, altering transmission windows and spatial range.pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih+1
Strong – Dhiman et al., 2010/2011, Indian J Med Res (climate–malaria sections).
Elevated mean temps accelerate Aedes life cycle and virus replication; optimum around ~29 °C gives maximal transmission
Moderate – Shilpi et al., ~2018, Scientific Reports–type climate model of chikungunya risk in
Temperature and rainfall influence Culex abundance and amplifying hosts (pigs, birds), modifying JE incidence and spatial distribution.pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih+1
based mapping of JE vs. temp/rainfall in an environmental health journal).
Sandfly vectors are temperature and humidity dependent; warming can shift/expand suitable habitats, implicated in re
Emerging–moderate – Dhiman et al., 2010, IJMR (section on kala
Higher temperatures increase bacterial growth and reduce water quality; often coincide with heavy rainfall/floods that contaminate water sources, increasing
Moderate–strong – Bhavnani et al., 2014, Global Environmental Change (“Climate change and waterborne diarrhoea in northern India”).
Same mechanisms; weekly temperature (15–30 °C) associated with higher prevalence of STEC and norovirus in Indian
Emerging–moderate – Bhavnani et al., 2014, Global Environmental Change (pathogen
Ambient temperature strongly influences bacterial multiplication along the food chain; multiple time
series show strong positive association of temperature with
Strong (global) – Lal et al., 2023, Int J Environ Res Public Health (“Impact of climate change on foodborne infections and intoxications”).
Vibrio and enteric pathogens thrive at higher temperatures; extreme rainfall/heat damage WASH infrastructure and contaminate water and
Emerging–moderate – India Health Fund synthesis drawing on multiple peer
Repeated occupational heat stress and dehydration in hot agricultural environments cause repeated subclinical kidney injury → chronic tubulo
Emerging–moderate – Johnson et al. / Wesseling et al., 2017, Kidney International Reports (“
in agricultural communities: A case against heat stress nephropathy?”).
Conceptually high but quantitatively limited; more suitable for exploratory EBOD.
Warming and humidity expand suitable habitat and activity for venomous snakes; altered monsoon and land
use patterns increase human–snake contact in rural India; more time outdoors at night in
Emerging–moderate – recent climate–snakebite hotspot modelling for India (peer
reviewed ecological models summarised in 2025 synthesis).
Promising but methodologically complex for EBOD; best as a scenario analysis.
Strong – Azhar et al., 2014, Environ Res; Gasparrini et al., 2015, Lancet.pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih+1
